input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A building or infrastructure asset within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, represented as a cyber-physical system through the integration of static models and runtime data to enable simulation, analysis, and optimization across the entire lifecycle from design to operational phases.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real-time insights, predictive analytics, and optimization services for infrastructure management in the AECO domain, supporting data-driven decision-making and stakeholder collaboration throughout the building lifecycle from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Centralized architecture with the DT Framework acting as a hub coordinating roles including Building Control Engineers (device modeling and deployment), Facility Managers (real-time monitoring and control), and DT Engineers (service integration); multiple digital twin instances managed via modular architecture with standardized extension points
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Modular architecture with standardized extension points supporting custom service and analytics integration, where the DT Framework serves as a central hub for bidirectional data exchange enabling holistic building life cycle management
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability across heterogeneous systems addressed through non-functional requirements (NF_X), with modular architecture and standardized extension points enabling integration of custom services and analytics
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework utilizes Building Information Modeling (BIM) as the foundational geometric model, enriched with semantic annotations for Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers) to establish a unified semantic foundation. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles are applied to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM assets, automatically generating deployable Digital Twin artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Data sources originate from BIM models, which serve as the primary input for transforming physical infrastructure parameters into digital representations, with Building Control Engineers defining device configurations within the BIM environment to structure the DT's data architecture.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Building Control Engineers define actuators (e.g., lighting systems) within BIM, with control signals fed back to the Building Control System (BCS) through specified control semantics for actuation mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are defined within BIM by Building Control Engineers, with runtime semantics including sensor configurations and post-occupancy sensor data collection.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model validity is assessed using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric to measure alignment between the Digital Twin and physical counterpart, with validation performed after model creation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time data exchange facilitated by Eclipse Hono and Ditto, transmitting sensor data and live operational data from physical assets to the digital twin for synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Bidirectional communication enabling control signals to be transmitted from the digital twin to physical Building Control Systems (BCS), with no specific technical details provided in the document regarding command types, formats, or validation mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Eclipse Hono and Ditto serve as the central hub for real-time data exchange, with no specific details on network topology, protocols, bandwidth, latency, or security protocols provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses initial device modeling within BIM environments where Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators, and controllers to address BIM's limitations in representing Building Control System (BCS) components. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles transform enriched BIM models into structured representations for automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts. Real-time data exchange ensures continuous synchronization between physical and digital twins, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for Facility Managers to monitor and control operations. The modular architecture with standardized extension points enables DT Engineers to integrate custom services and analytics, facilitating lifecycle governance through role-specific responsibilities.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process utilizes Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is managed through a modular architecture featuring standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, allowing the DT to adapt to changing operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system supports real-time monitoring and control via interactive visualization interfaces for Facility Managers. Custom analytics services are integrated through modular extension points, though specific insight generation methodologies, processing capabilities, or decision-making confidence measures are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework adheres to Building Information Modeling (BIM) standards as the foundational specification for device modeling and semantic representation, ensuring compatibility with AECO-specific systems and standards for construction project complexity requirements.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the definition and scope of the system described in the introduction. It correctly identifies the domain (AECO), the nature of the asset (cyber-physical, e.g., a building), and the integration method (static models with runtime data). This is supported by the text: 'Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization'.

The extraction precisely lists the services mentioned in the text, such as real-time insights, predictive analytics, and optimization, as well as the goal of stakeholder collaboration across the building lifecycle. This is supported by the source: 'DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities' and 'improved stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation'.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific technologies mentioned in the text—Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and Unreal Engine—and accurately describes their roles in the framework. Quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the real-time synchronization aspect mentioned in the source. Supported by quotes: 'Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins' and 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The description correctly identifies the centralized architecture and the three roles (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) as outlined in Table I. The DT Framework is described as a central hub in Section III-B. However, 'multiple digital twin instances' isn't explicitly mentioned in the provided text - the document discusses the framework's architecture but doesn't specifically address managing multiple twin instances. Supported by quotes: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange' and Table I which details the three roles.

The description comprehensively captures the orchestration and coordination mechanisms. Supported by multiple quotes: 'A modular architecture with defined extension points to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in AECO', 'The framework's modular architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the integration of custom services and analytics', and 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management'.

The description accurately reflects the approach to interoperability and integration. Supported by quotes: 'Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements (NFX) address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems' and 'The framework's modular architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the integration of custom services and analytics'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and detailed, accurately reflecting the source text's description of the model-driven framework. It correctly identifies BIM as the foundational geometric model, the enrichment with BCS components, the use of MDE principles for extraction, and the role of Building Control Engineers. Supported by quotes: "model-driven framework... grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)", "The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models", "automatically generate deployable DT artifacts".

The description correctly identifies how actuators are defined by Building Control Engineers within BIM and provides a specific example from the text (lighting systems). It also accurately describes the feedback loop to the BCS. Supported by: 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'control semantics for lighting systems directly into BIM models', 'feed control signals back to the BCS'.

The extraction accurately combines the modeling of sensors in BIM with the runtime semantics and data collection strategies mentioned in the context of the BOREALIS project. Supported by: 'runtime semantics, such as sensor configurations... embedded into BIM models', 'BOREALIS... using post-occupancy sensor data', 'Building Control Engineers... define sensors... within the BIM environment'.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific metric (ODTE) used for validation and its purpose in measuring alignment between the digital and physical counterparts. Supported by: 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric', 'measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
